summary: Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood co-founder, best known for Ophelia (Elizabeth Siddal in a cold bathtub) and the controversial Christ in the House of His Parents (red-headed boy in a carpentry shop). Other notable works include The Order of Release, Peace Concluded, Bubbles (sold to Pears Soap), The North-West Passage, The Boyhood of Raleigh, The Blind Girl, and Mariana. Married Effie Gray after her unconsummated marriage to John Ruskin.

works.name: Ophelia (1851–1852)
works.description: Depicts Shakespeare's Ophelia floating/singing in a stream as she drowns, surrounded by brightly colored flowers. Long purple flowers represent 'dead men's fingers'; a chain of violets around her neck; a bright poppy symbolizing death by her hand. Elizabeth Siddal posed in a cold bathtub; Millais had to pay her 50-pound doctor's bill. Landscape inspired by the Hogsmill River (artist worked beside Holman Hunt painting The Hireling Shepherd). Originally featured a water vole; John Ruskin described the setting as a 'rascally wirefenced garden-rolled-nursery-maid's paradise.' A botany professor used the detailed flora to instruct students. Displayed at the Royal Academy alongside Arthur Hughes's painting subtitled 'And He Will Not Come Back Again.'

clues.clue: Depicts Ophelia floating/singing in a stream/river before drowning, holding scattered bouquet of flowers

works.name: Christ in the House of His Parents (1849–1850)
works.description: Controversial painting depicting Jesus as a red-headed boy in a lower-class carpentry shop with wood shavings on the floor. The boy holds up a bleeding hand and kisses his mother, who was modeled on Mary Hodgkinson. A boy holding a water bowl (John the Baptist) stares at the figure. A dove on a ladder represents the Holy Spirit. Charles Dickens attacked the depiction of Mary as 'so horrible in her ugliness' as to appear 'in the vilest cabaret in France, or the lowest ginshop in England'; ironically, this criticism boosted the PRB's popularity. John Ruskin defended it. William Holman Hunt created a companion piece.

clues.clue: Controversial carpentry shop scene: red-headed boy Jesus holds up bleeding hand, kisses mother (modeled on Mary Hodgkinson); wood shavings on the floor
examples: sparked a controversy over realism with a painting in which wood shavings litter the floor of a carpentry shop where a red-headed boy is tended to by his parents

clues.clue: A dove on a ladder in the background represents the Holy Spirit

works.description: A woman hands the title document to a British soldier/guard as her wounded husband (arm in sling) embraces her while a dog jumps up on him. Effie Gray posed for it.

works.name: Peace Concluded (1856)
works.description: Two girls play with toy animals as their father holds a newspaper announcing the end of the Crimean War. Effie Gray posed for it.

works.name: Bubbles (1886)
works.description: Depicts a child looking up in wonder as he blows soap bubbles, a commercialization of the Dutch Homo Bulla motif (transience of life). The model was Millais's grandson, Sir William Milbourne James, later an Admiral and grandson of Effie Gray. Millais sold the painting to Thomas J. Barratt of Pears Soap, who added a bar of Pears soap to it for use in advertisements — drawing widespread ire from critics. The painting is now housed at the Lady Lever Art Gallery in Port Sunlight, the model village founded by Lever Brothers (later Unilever) to advertise its soap business.

clues.clue: Child looking up in wonder blowing bubbles; the model was Millais's grandson, Sir William James, later an Admiral

works.name: The North-West Passage (1874)
works.description: Depicts the aged adventurer Edward Trelawny seated as a wizened sailor being read old logbooks by his great-granddaughter, with the subtitle 'It might be done and England should do it.' The teetotaler Trelawny was outraged that Millais included a glass of grog in the painting. The pose of the central patriarch in George Bernard Shaw's Heartbreak House (Captain Shotover) was modeled on this painting.

clues.clue: Edward Trelawny depicted as a wizened sailor being read old logbooks by his great-granddaughter; subtitle 'It might be done and England should do it'

works.name: The Boyhood of Raleigh (1870)
works.description: Heroicizes the British Empire with a depiction of a young Walter Raleigh sitting on a beach listening to an old sailor's tales. James Marsh's cover for the Talk Talk album It's My Life deconstructs the painting.

works.name: The Blind Girl (1856)
works.description: Depicts an accordion-carrying blind girl seated in the open air, unable to see a double rainbow above the houses in the background, while the child in her clutches gazes back at it. A butterfly rests on her red cloak.

works.name: Mariana (1851)
works.description: Inspired by Tennyson's poem (itself based on a character from Shakespeare's Measure for Measure). Depicts a woman in a blue robe stretching backwards with her hands on her hips in front of a stained-glass Annunciation window copied from the chapel of Merton College, Oxford. An image symbolizing St. Agnes' Eve appears inside a shield on the stained-glass window.

clues.clue: Woman in blue robe with hands on hips in front of a stained-glass Annunciation copied from the Merton College chapel; inspired by Tennyson

this poet who inspired Pre-Raphaelite paintings like John Everett Millais's Mariana

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Includes The Ruling Passion (a reclining ornithologist showing a stuffed bird of paradise to children), Sir Isumbras at the Ford (parodied by Frederic Sandys), The Eve of St. Agnes (a woman undressing), Pizarro Seizing the Inca of Peru (man grasping at a leopard), The Black Brunswicker (Napoleon Crossing the Alps visible in the background; set at a Brussels ball on the eve of Quatre Bras), The Ransom (a knight hands over a collection of jewels to secure the safety of his two daughters), The Last Trek (death of a hunter), portraits of John Ruskin standing in front of a waterfall in Glenfinlas and an unfinished portrait of Thomas Carlyle (attacked by suffragette Anne Hunt in protest of Emmeline Pankhurst's arrest), and portraits of Cardinal John Henry Newman and the Princes in the Tower. Illustrated Trollope's Framley Parsonage. Painted a series of fairy paintings inspired by The Tempest.

comprehensive_summary:
John Everett Millais (1829–1896) was an English painter and co-founder of the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood alongside Dante Gabriel Rossetti and William Holman Hunt. The Brotherhood published their ideas in the periodical The Germ and signed works with cryptic initials (as in Isabella). They sought to restore artistic aesthetics to those that existed before the Renaissance painter Raphael — critics like Reynolds ('Sir Sloshua') were mocked for their Raphael worship.

Ophelia (1851–52) depicts Shakespeare's character floating in a stream, hands held face-up, as she drowns, surrounded by meticulously detailed flowers. Long purple flowers represent 'dead men's fingers' in the corner; a chain of violets circles her neck; poppies symbolize death. The flora was so detailed a botany professor used the painting to instruct students. Elizabeth Siddal posed in a cold bathtub lit by lamps; Millais was forced to pay a 50-pound doctor's bill after she fell ill. The landscape was painted along the Hogsmill River, where Millais worked alongside Holman Hunt painting The Hireling Shepherd. John Ruskin described the setting as a 'rascally wirefenced garden-rolled-nursery-maid's paradise' and noted it originally featured a water vole. The painting was displayed at the Royal Academy alongside Arthur Hughes's painting of Ophelia subtitled 'And He Will Not Come Back Again.' Lars von Trier's promotional poster for Melancholia recreates the composition with Kirsten Dunst lying in a wedding dress holding a bouquet.

Christ in the House of His Parents (1849–50) controversially depicted Jesus as a red-headed boy in a grimy lower-class carpentry shop with wood shavings on the floor. The boy holds up a bleeding hand (cut on an exposed nail) and kisses his mother (modeled on Mary Hodgkinson); John the Baptist brings water in a bowl; a dove on a ladder represents the Holy Spirit. Charles Dickens attacked the depiction of Mary as 'so horrible in her ugliness' as to appear 'in the vilest cabaret in France, or the lowest ginshop in England' — ironically, this criticism boosted the PRB's popularity. John Ruskin defended it. William Holman Hunt created a companion piece, A Converted British Family Sheltering a Christian Missionary from the Persecution of the Druids.

Millais married Effie Gray after her unconsummated marriage to Ruskin. She posed for The Order of Release (a woman handing the title document to a British soldier while her wounded husband with arm in sling embraces her and a dog jumps up) and Peace Concluded (two girls play with toy animals while their father holds a newspaper announcing the end of the Crimean War).

Bubbles (1886) depicts a child — Millais's grandson Sir William James, later an Admiral — looking up in wonder while blowing soap bubbles, an homage to the Dutch Homo Bulla motif. Millais sold the picture to Thomas J. Barratt of Pears Soap, who added a bar of Pears soap and used it in advertisements; the painting now hangs at the Lady Lever Art Gallery in Port Sunlight, the model village built by Lever Brothers (later Unilever). The North-West Passage (1874) shows the aged adventurer Edward Trelawny seated as a wizened sailor being read old logbooks by his great-granddaughter, subtitled 'It might be done and England should do it.' The teetotaler Trelawny was outraged by Millais's inclusion of a glass of grog. Captain Shotover, the central patriarch of George Bernard Shaw's Heartbreak House, is shown seated in the same pose. The Boyhood of Raleigh (1870) shows a young Walter Raleigh listening to an old sailor's tales on the beach; James Marsh's cover for the Talk Talk album It's My Life deconstructs the painting. The Blind Girl (1856) features an accordion-carrying blind girl who cannot see a double rainbow above the houses behind her, while the child in her clutches gazes back at it; a butterfly rests on her red cloak. Mariana (1851), inspired by Tennyson, shows a woman in a blue robe stretching backwards in front of a stained-glass Annunciation copied from the chapel of Merton College, Oxford; an image symbolizing St. Agnes' Eve appears inside a shield on the window.

Other notable works include The Ruling Passion (a reclining ornithologist showing a stuffed bird of paradise to children), Sir Isumbras at the Ford (parodied by Frederic Sandys), The Eve of St. Agnes (a woman undressing), Pizarro Seizing the Inca of Peru (a man grasping at a leopard), The Black Brunswicker (Napoleon Crossing the Alps visible in the background; set at the Duchess of Richmond's Brussels ball on the eve of Quatre Bras), The Ransom (a knight handing over jewels for the safety of his two daughters), The Last Trek (the death of a hunter), and a series of fairy paintings inspired by The Tempest. He painted portraits of John Ruskin standing by a waterfall in Glenfinlas, Cardinal John Henry Newman, and the historical Princes in the Tower; his unfinished portrait of Thomas Carlyle was attacked by suffragette Anne Hunt in protest of Emmeline Pankhurst's arrest. He illustrated Trollope's Framley Parsonage. He frequently hired Fanny Eaton, daughter of a Jamaican slave, as a model, and Dorothy Dene (who modeled for Leighton's Flaming June) also posed for him.
